<insights>

- Isolated files — no import edges between 7 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
- Isolated files — no import edges between 7 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
- Separate the domain models (AppearanceSettings, Schedule, Validation) into their own explicit zone by ensuring Swift module imports are resolved so community detection can distinguish them from project configuration files.
- Exclude non-source assets (Makefile, LICENSE, .gitignore) from zone analysis inputs — they have no import edges and will always anchor to whichever zone lacks a stronger attractor.
- Validation.swift is classified as 'schema' alongside 'model' files; verify it contains only structural constraints and not business logic that belongs in a service layer.
- Project configuration files (Makefile, LICENSE, .gitignore) are merged with core Swift domain models due to shared absence of import edges, obscuring the models' architectural role.
- AppearanceSettings embeds WCAG contrast and luminance calculations as pure logic — this means display correctness logic lives in the persistence model layer rather than a dedicated presentation or accessibility service, which is pragmatic for a small app but will require extraction if theming ever becomes more complex.
- Validation.swift is an extension on Schedule rather than a standalone validator type — this collapses the schema and domain model into the same type, making it impossible to validate a schedule without instantiating one.
- Validation logic is expressed as a Schedule extension rather than an injectable validator, coupling the domain model to its own schema enforcement and preventing alternative validation strategies (e.g., stricter import-time vs. editor-time rules) without modifying the model itself.

</insights>
